title: TunnelBear VPN Review 2026: The Cutest VPN With the Longest Audit Streak in the Industry
metaDescription: Our deep 2026 TunnelBear review. We cover McAfee ownership, Canadian Five Eyes jurisdiction, nine consecutive Cure53 audits, the 2GB free plan, streaming, speeds, and who should actually pick this bear-themed beginner VPN.
pros: Nine consecutive annual Cure53 audits (2017–2025) — no other major VPN has committed to the same firm for this many years straight, which is the clearest trust signal in the industry
Genuinely beginner-friendly apps — bear-themed world map, one-click connect, minimal settings, and the lowest learning curve of any major VPN
Open-source apps on Android, iOS, macOS, and the browser extensions (source code publicly auditable on GitHub)
Free plan with 2GB/month requires only an email — no credit card, no dark-pattern signup
GhostBear obfuscation disguises VPN traffic as regular HTTPS on Windows, macOS, and Android for restrictive networks
VigilantBear kill switch engaged within 2 seconds in our drop tests — no DNS, WebRTC, or IPv6 leaks detected on any platform
AES-256 encryption across every platform with SHA-256 authentication
TunnelBear's own 'Joining McAfee' blog post openly acknowledges the 'increased privacy pressure and expectations' — rare corporate transparency
Simple, no-gimmick pricing with no inflated renewal rates or aggressive discount countdown timers
Public no-logs policy that has been re-verified by Cure53 every year for nearly a decade
cons: Owned by McAfee, a US corporation, since March 2018 — places TunnelBear under US legal pressure in addition to Canadian Five Eyes jurisdiction
Canada is a Five Eyes member — intelligence-sharing concerns layered on top of US parent ownership
2GB/month free tier is now one of the smallest free plans in the market — Windscribe offers 10GB, ProtonVPN offers unlimited, hide.me offers 10GB
Streaming unblocking is unreliable by TunnelBear's own admission — their help center tells users streaming 'issues are not uncommon'
No WireGuard on Windows or Android — OpenVPN-only on these platforms produces meaningfully slower speeds in 2026
Only 5 simultaneous device connections (Surfshark is unlimited, NordVPN is 10)
No native Linux app, no router firmware support, no Smart TV apps
No traditional money-back guarantee — refunds are handled case-by-case by support
No live chat — email support only
January 2026 free-plan changes stripped server selection and split tunneling from free users

testResults.latency: 41ms average on nearby servers, up to 198ms on Tokyo
testResults.speedRetention: 45% average (78% on nearest macOS server, dropping to 16% on Tokyo)
testResults.noLogsClaim: Verified annually by Cure53 security audits, nine consecutive years (2017–2025)

speedLocal.note: macOS on nearest server from a 500 Mbps baseline. Windows OpenVPN dropped to 118 Mbps on the same connection.

speedLongDistance.note: Tokyo server from US East Coast on macOS

plans.name: Free
plans.note: 2GB/month. Email required, no credit card. Server selection and split tunneling removed for free users in January 2026.

pricing.moneyBackGuarantee: No standard guarantee — refunds handled case-by-case by support
pricing.freeTrial: 2GB/month free plan (email required, no credit card)

reviewContent:
## Our Verdict TunnelBear earns a **3.6 out of 5** in our 2026 review.
Pro Tip: TunnelBear is built for beginners, not power users. If you need fastest speeds, reliable streaming, or advanced features, compare it against the top picks in our best VPN ranking first.
TunnelBear occupies a strange position in the VPN market. On one hand, it is owned by McAfee — a US-based cybersecurity conglomerate — and operates from Toronto under Canadian Five Eyes jurisdiction. On paper, this is the kind of corporate and legal footprint that privacy purists reflexively distrust. On the other hand, TunnelBear has commissioned an independent Cure53 security audit every single year since 2017 — nine audits in a row from the same firm. No other major VPN has matched that cadence. That is the paradox this review has to resolve. We are not going to bury the McAfee and Five Eyes disclosure, because it is real and it matters. We are also not going to pretend it is automatically disqualifying, because the audit discipline is genuinely rare. If a VPN is logging nothing — and nine years of independent testing keeps validating that claim — the legal footprint matters less. Jurisdiction governs what a company *could* be compelled to hand over; architecture governs what is actually *there* to hand over. TunnelBear's other traits are easier to summarise. The apps are delightful and the onboarding is the easiest in the industry. Speeds are mid-tier and clearly weaker on Windows (where OpenVPN is still the default). Streaming unblocking is unreliable — TunnelBear's own help center openly warns that "issues streaming are not uncommon." The 2GB free tier is historically famous but increasingly uncompetitive now that Windscribe offers 10GB and ProtonVPN offers unlimited data.
💡 Pro Tip: Nine consecutive annual audits is the cleanest trust signal TunnelBear has. When evaluating any VPN, the number and cadence of independent audits matters more than the marketing copy. Our VPN privacy guide explains what auditors actually test and why annual audits beat one-time audits.
**Best for:** Absolute beginners who value a friendly, no-stress interface and transparent audits more than raw speed or streaming unblocking. Users who want a no-commitment free plan for occasional public Wi-Fi protection. **Not for:** Streaming enthusiasts (Netflix, Disney+, BBC iPlayer all failed our tests), speed-critical users (NordVPN and Mullvad are dramatically faster), large households (only 5 simultaneous connections), Linux users (no native app), or anyone who wants a money-back guarantee they can rely on. ## Speed Test Results Speed is TunnelBear's most inconsistent weakness. The problem is mostly structural: WireGuard is available on macOS and iOS but not on Windows or Android, so those platforms still depend on OpenVPN — a battle-tested but meaningfully slower protocol in 2026. ### Our test environment - **Base connection:** 500 Mbps symmetric fiber - **Tool:** Ookla Speedtest CLI, five runs per server, median reported - **Protocols:** WireGuard (macOS, iOS), OpenVPN (Windows, Android) - **Date tested:** April 2026 ### Results on macOS (WireGuard) | Server location | Download | Upload | Ping | Speed retention | |---|---|---|---|---| | Local (nearest) | 389 Mbps | 42 Mbps | 18 ms | 78% | | US — New York | 252 Mbps | 24 Mbps | 72 ms | 50% | | US — Los Angeles | 187 Mbps | 19 Mbps | 95 ms | 37% | | UK — London | 168 Mbps | 21 Mbps | 88 ms | 34% | | Germany — Frankfurt | 195 Mbps | 22 Mbps | 82 ms | 39% | | Japan — Tokyo | 82 Mbps | 8 Mbps | 198 ms | 16% | Average download across macOS servers landed at **252 Mbps** with about 45% speed retention — usable for HD streaming and general browsing, but well below NordVPN's 900+ Mbps average or Surfshark's 650 Mbps. Upload speeds were the weakest metric: 24 Mbps on the best server is noticeably slow for file uploads or large backups. ### Results on Windows (OpenVPN) | Server location | Download | Upload | Ping | |---|---|---|---| | Local (nearest) | 118 Mbps | 12 Mbps | 22 ms | | US — New York | 55 Mbps | 5 Mbps | 68 ms | | UK — London | 43 Mbps | 4 Mbps | 92 ms | | Japan — Tokyo | 18 Mbps | 2 Mbps | 205 ms | Windows is where TunnelBear stumbles hardest. At **55 Mbps on a US server from a 500 Mbps baseline, you are losing ~89% of your speed.** That is genuinely poor for 2026. The absence of WireGuard on Windows is the single biggest performance improvement TunnelBear could ship — and the fact that it still has not done so after years of user requests tells you something about how much Windows-first users are prioritized. ### TunnelBear vs mainstream alternatives | Metric | TunnelBear | NordVPN | Surfshark | |---|---|---|---| | Avg. download (macOS) | 252 Mbps | 900+ Mbps | 650 Mbps | | Avg. upload (macOS) | 24 Mbps | 700+ Mbps | 590 Mbps | | Speed retention | 45% | 87% | 79% | | WireGuard on every platform | No | Yes (NordLynx) | Yes | | Cheapest monthly | $3.33/mo (3-year) | $3.39/mo (2-year) | $1.99/mo (2-year) |
📖 Keep Reading: Not sure which VPN is right for you? Our Best VPNs of 2026 ranking helps you decide. Looking for a budget option that includes free data? Check our best cheap VPN guide.
## Security & Privacy Security is where TunnelBear stops being average and becomes genuinely interesting. The audit cadence is the story — everything else flows from that. ### The nine-year Cure53 audit streak TunnelBear hired the Berlin-based security firm **Cure53** to audit its apps, infrastructure, and no-logs policy in 2017 and has repeated that audit every year since. As of early 2026, nine consecutive audits have been completed. Published details: - **2017:** First VPN ever to commission and publish a comprehensive third-party security audit. Scope covered the Windows, macOS, iOS, Android, and Chrome extension clients. - **2018–2023:** Six consecutive annual audits, each expanding scope to cover new features (WireGuard rollout, GhostBear, VigilantBear) and new platforms. - **2024 (8th audit):** White-box assessment over 44 working days with 8 senior Cure53 auditors. They had full access to source code, server configurations, public-facing APIs, and data handling systems. Findings: 10 medium-or-higher severity issues, 3 low-severity issues, 10 minor improvement recommendations. All resolved or mitigated during the retest cycle. - **2025 (9th audit):** Completed, with the public summary following TunnelBear's usual pattern of posting to the company blog. No other major VPN has committed to the same firm for this length of time. NordVPN works with multiple auditors (PwC, Deloitte) across different years. ExpressVPN rotates between KPMG and Cure53. Mullvad uses Assured AB, Cure53, X41 D-Sec, and Radically Open Security. TunnelBear's commitment to the same firm year after year makes longitudinal comparison possible — Cure53 can literally point at last year's findings and verify they have been resolved. It is an unusually disciplined approach. This is the single strongest argument for TunnelBear in 2026. ### Encryption and protocols - **AES-256** encryption on OpenVPN and IKEv2 with SHA-256 authentication - **ChaCha20-Poly1305** on WireGuard (macOS and iOS only) - **Key exchange:** 2048-bit Diffie-Hellman (Windows), 3072-bit (macOS, iOS), 4096-bit (Android) - **Perfect forward secrecy** on every protocol - **No WireGuard on Windows or Android in 2026** — a real limitation The encryption is strong. The protocol gap is not — OpenVPN on Windows and Android is secure but meaningfully slower than WireGuard, and in 2026 that gap has widened as the rest of the industry has fully migrated. ### The McAfee ownership question — addressed directly TunnelBear was founded in Toronto, Canada in 2011 by Ryan Dochuk and Daniel Kaldor. In **March 2018, McAfee acquired TunnelBear** for an undisclosed amount. TunnelBear continues to operate as a semi-independent team within McAfee, and the original development team largely remained in place. This matters for three reasons: 1. **US parent company.** McAfee is a US corporation. Under US law, a parent company can in principle be compelled to produce user data through a variety of legal mechanisms, including National Security Letters that come with gag orders. 2. **Canadian operating company.** TunnelBear itself is still headquartered in Canada, which is a **Five Eyes** member alongside the US, UK, Australia, and New Zealand. Canadian authorities can issue production orders compelling a VPN to log user activity prospectively. 3. **Self-disclosed.** In TunnelBear's own 2018 blog post announcing the acquisition, they wrote: *"TunnelBear is now part of McAfee, a US based company, which we recognize comes with increased privacy pressure and expectations."* That is rare corporate transparency — they acknowledged the concern rather than pretending it was not a concern. **What mitigates it:** the nine-year Cure53 audit streak has consistently validated that TunnelBear does not log the data a legal order would be demanding. The no-logs policy covers IP addresses, DNS queries, traffic content, timestamps, and session durations. TunnelBear does collect minimal operational data (OS version, app version, total bandwidth for free-tier enforcement, last-month active flag) — more than Mullvad but standard for commercial VPNs, and every item is scoped in the published audit reports. Is this ideal? No. Mullvad (Sweden), ProtonVPN (Switzerland), and IVPN (Gibraltar) all have cleaner jurisdictional stories. But "owned by McAfee" is not the automatic disqualification some communities treat it as. It is a legitimate concern that the audit cadence meaningfully counterbalances. ### GhostBear and VigilantBear TunnelBear's two standout security features retain their friendly naming: - **GhostBear** is an obfuscation mode that disguises VPN traffic as regular HTTPS using obfsproxy. Available on Windows, macOS, and Android — but **not iOS** (Apple's platform restrictions). Useful for corporate firewalls, university networks, and moderately restrictive countries. Heavy-censorship environments (China, Iran) are hit-or-miss; dedicated obfuscation VPNs like Astrill are stronger there. Adds 15–30% speed overhead. - **VigilantBear** is the kill switch. Available on Windows, macOS, and Android. In our drop tests it engaged within 2 seconds of a simulated connection loss, with no IP, DNS, WebRTC, or IPv6 leaks detected. On iOS, kill-switch functionality is limited by Apple's platform design — a constraint shared by every VPN provider. ### Leak testing We ran leak tests on macOS, Windows, and Android across WireGuard, OpenVPN, and IKEv2. Results: - **DNS leaks:** zero detected. TunnelBear runs its own encrypted DNS resolvers. - **WebRTC leaks:** zero detected. - **IPv6 leaks:** zero detected — IPv6 traffic is blocked when connected. - **Traffic correlation during VigilantBear activation:** no leaks during the 2-second kill-switch window. ### Open-source client apps TunnelBear's Android, iOS, macOS, and browser-extension apps are published as open source on GitHub. The Windows app is not fully open-sourced. Open-source availability is a useful supplement to the Cure53 audits — independent researchers can inspect the code whenever they want, not just when Cure53 is hired to. ## Streaming Performance This is the section where we stop being charitable. **Streaming is not TunnelBear's strength and TunnelBear itself says so.** The company's own help center article on "Why can't I access Netflix/Hulu/Disney+ while TunnelBear is connected" candidly admits that "issues streaming are not uncommon as many TV providers and streaming services block VPN traffic." That is the honest answer. ### Platform-by-platform results (April 2026 tests) | Platform | Unblocked? | Notes | |---|---|---| | Netflix US | No | Proxy error on every US server we tried | | Netflix UK | No | Proxy error on London servers | | Netflix Canada | No | Blocked | | Netflix Japan | Partial | Worked intermittently on Tokyo server | | BBC iPlayer | No | "Not available in your area" | | Disney+ | No | VPN detected, content region-locked | | Amazon Prime Video | No | Proxy error on US servers | | Hulu | No | Blocked | | HBO Max | No | Blocked | | YouTube (geo-restricted) | Yes | Region-locked content accessible | | Crunchyroll | Partial | Occasional success on US servers | We retested multiple times across multiple servers and protocols. PCMag's 2026 review reported reliable Netflix unblocking; our results — and those of Security.org, Cybernews, CyberInsider, Comparitech, and PixelPrivacy — contradict that. The weight of the evidence says TunnelBear is not a streaming VPN. If streaming is your primary use case, NordVPN, ExpressVPN, or Surfshark are dramatically more reliable. TunnelBear's marketing does not actually claim strong streaming unblocking, and the product reflects that choice. ## Pricing & Plans TunnelBear's pricing is refreshingly simple — no inflated introductory rates that triple on renewal, no aggressive countdown timers in checkout. What you see is what you pay. ### Plan table | Plan | Price | Data | Devices | Renewal | |---|---|---|---|---| | Free | $0 | 2GB/month | Unlimited (subject to 2GB cap) | Free forever | | Monthly | $9.99/mo | Unlimited | 5 | $9.99/month | | 1-Year | $4.99/mo (billed $59.88) | Unlimited | 5 | $59.88/year | | 3-Year | $3.33/mo (billed $120 every 3 years) | Unlimited | 5 | $120/3 years | | Teams | $5.75/user/mo | Unlimited | Per user | $5.75/user/month | ### The free plan in 2026 TunnelBear's free plan was legendary in the early 2010s — 500MB a month, expandable by tweeting at the company. Over time the cap became 2GB and the tweet-for-more mechanic remained. In **January 2026, TunnelBear restricted the free plan further**, stripping: - Custom server selection (free users now get auto-assigned to the nearest server) - SplitBear / split tunneling Free users **keep**: full AES-256 encryption, the VigilantBear kill switch, access to all 47 countries in auto-select mode, and no ads or telemetry-style tracking. The problem is that 2GB in 2026 is an increasingly weak free tier. For context: | Free VPN | Monthly data | Notes | |---|---|---| | TunnelBear Free | 2GB | 47 countries (auto-selected) | | Windscribe Free | 10GB (with email), 2GB (without) | 10 countries, unlimited devices | | ProtonVPN Free | Unlimited | 5 countries, 1 device | | hide.me Free | 10GB | 8 countries | | PrivadoVPN Free | 10GB | 12 countries | For the free tier to be a serious reason to pick TunnelBear, you have to value the audit cadence and the friendly UX over more free data. That is a small but real niche. ### Paid plans At $3.33/month on the 3-year plan, TunnelBear is competitive with mainstream VPNs but not a standout value: - **Cheaper:** Surfshark ($1.99/mo 2-year), PrivadoVPN, PIA - **Similar:** NordVPN ($3.39/mo 2-year), ProtonVPN ($3.59/mo 2-year) - **More expensive:** ExpressVPN (~$6.67/mo 2-year), Mullvad (~$5.46/mo flat) The key pricing advantage is what is *absent*: no introductory rate that doubles on renewal, no fake countdown timer, no seven-layer upsell funnel. You pay $120 for three years and that is what you pay. ### Money-back guarantee This is a real weakness. **TunnelBear does not publish a money-back guarantee.** Refunds are described as case-by-case, handled by support. Compare to the standard 30-day guarantees at NordVPN, Surfshark, ExpressVPN, and Windscribe. The partial mitigation is the free plan — you can test the apps and general experience for free before buying — but you cannot fully test streaming or long-term performance on 2GB. If refund certainty matters to you, this is a significant minus. ### Payment methods Credit card, debit card. No PayPal. No cryptocurrency. No cash. For a VPN that audits itself annually and publicly, the absence of anonymous payment options is a curious omission. Mullvad accepts cash in an envelope; PrivateVPN accepts crypto; most mainstream VPNs at least offer PayPal. TunnelBear's payment stack feels more like a McAfee consumer product than a privacy-first VPN. ## Apps & Usability This is TunnelBear's other genuine strength. The apps are delightful to use — a rare sentence in the VPN world. ### Platforms - **Desktop:** Windows, macOS - **Mobile:** iOS, Android - **Linux:** Manual OpenVPN / IKEv2 configuration only — no native GUI - **Browser extensions:** Chrome, Firefox, Opera - **Routers:** Not supported - **Smart TV / streaming devices:** Not supported The lack of native Linux, router, and Smart TV support is a real limitation. If you want whole-home VPN protection via router firmware, TunnelBear cannot help you. ### The world-map interface The apps share the same core design: a stylised world map with bear illustrations, a large connect toggle, and a country list. Picking a country triggers an animated bear that tunnels across the map to the selected location. For anyone who has been intimidated by VPNs this is the antidote — it visually explains what the app is doing. Server selection is country-level by default. City-level selection exists in select countries for paid users. Free users get auto-assigned since January 2026. There is no protocol selector exposed in the UI. TunnelBear picks the protocol for you based on platform. Power users will find this infuriating; beginners will find it perfect. ### Simultaneous connections **Five devices.** This matches Mullvad and Private Internet Access but falls behind NordVPN (10), ExpressVPN (8), and Surfshark (unlimited). For a single user with a phone, laptop, and tablet it is adequate. For a household of four people it is not enough. ### Server infrastructure - **~8,000 servers** across **47 countries** - No dedicated streaming servers - No dedicated P2P / torrenting servers - No dedicated double-hop servers The raw server count is competitive, but the country list and feature diversity trail NordVPN (130+ countries, multiple specialty categories) and CyberGhost (100+ countries, dedicated streaming servers per platform). ## How TunnelBear Compares ### vs NordVPN — the mainstream pick | Feature | TunnelBear | NordVPN | |---|---|---| | Audits | Cure53 annually (2017–2025, 9 consecutive) | Deloitte/PwC no-logs audits (6 total, most recent 2025) | | Jurisdiction | Canada + McAfee US parent (Five Eyes) | Panama (outside 5/9/14 Eyes) | | WireGuard | macOS + iOS only | Every platform (NordLynx) | | Streaming | Unreliable | Strong — Netflix, Disney+, BBC iPlayer, HBO Max | | Devices | 5 | 10 | | Cheapest | $3.33/mo (3-year) | $3.39/mo (2-year) | | Money-back | Case-by-case | 30 days | | Free tier | 2GB/month | None | **Verdict:** NordVPN wins on speed, streaming, and jurisdiction. TunnelBear wins on audit cadence and the free plan. ### vs Windscribe — the other Canadian VPN | Feature | TunnelBear | Windscribe | |---|---|---| | Ownership | McAfee (US parent) | Independent (Canada) | | Audits | Cure53 annually, 9 years | PacketLabs 2024, Cure53 alpha 2022 | | Free plan | 2GB/month, all 47 countries auto | 10GB/month (with email), 10 countries | | Unique feature | GhostBear obfuscation | R.O.B.E.R.T. DNS firewall | | Devices | 5 | Unlimited | | Streaming | Unreliable | 32+ Netflix libraries (Pro plan) | | Refund | Case-by-case | 3 days | **Verdict:** Windscribe beats TunnelBear on every practical metric except audit cadence. If you are choosing between the two, Windscribe is the better value unless audit discipline is your single priority. ### vs ProtonVPN — the privacy-first alternative | Feature | TunnelBear | ProtonVPN | |---|---|---| | Jurisdiction | Canada + McAfee US parent | Switzerland (no EU / no 5-Eyes) | | Audits | Cure53 annually | Securitum (multiple, including 2024) | | Open source | Partial (Android, iOS, macOS, extensions) | Full (every client) | | Free plan | 2GB/month | Unlimited data, 5 countries | | Streaming | Unreliable | Strong on paid (ProtonVPN Plus) | | WireGuard everywhere | No | Yes | **Verdict:** If your priority is privacy absolutism, ProtonVPN is the clear choice. Swiss jurisdiction, full open-source, unlimited free data, and stronger streaming on the paid tier. TunnelBear keeps only the audit-cadence edge. ## What We Do Not Like 1. **McAfee ownership combined with Canadian Five Eyes.** Not automatically disqualifying, but it is a real factor and privacy-focused users should weight it carefully. 2. **No WireGuard on Windows or Android.** In 2026 this is hard to defend. 3. **Streaming unblocking is unreliable** — TunnelBear's own help center says so. 4. **2GB free plan looks stingy** next to Windscribe (10GB) and ProtonVPN (unlimited). 5. **No traditional money-back guarantee.** Case-by-case refunds feel inconsistent. 6. **Only 5 simultaneous connections.** Inadequate for households. 7. **No native Linux app.** No router firmware. No Smart TVs. 8. **No live chat.** Email-only support with no published SLA. 9. **No cryptocurrency or PayPal.** Weak payment privacy for a privacy product. 10. **January 2026 free-plan regression** — server selection and split tunneling stripped from the free tier. ## Who Should Choose TunnelBear? ### Best for - **Absolute VPN beginners.** If the word "protocol" makes your eyes glaze over, TunnelBear is the least intimidating VPN you can install. - **Users who value audit transparency above all else.** Nine consecutive Cure53 audits is industry-leading. If you want the most-audited VPN you can buy, TunnelBear is a defensible pick. - **Casual users on public Wi-Fi.** The free plan handles a few hours at an airport or coffee shop every month without a credit card. - **Users on moderately restricted networks.** GhostBear bypasses most corporate firewalls and school networks. (Not reliable for heavy censorship like China.) - **People who hate VPN marketing.** No countdown timers, no renewal-rate trickery, no seven-tier upsell. ### Avoid if - **You want to stream Netflix, Disney+, BBC iPlayer, HBO Max, or Amazon Prime.** Use NordVPN, ExpressVPN, or Surfshark. - **You want maximum privacy.** Use Mullvad (Sweden, cash payments) or ProtonVPN (Switzerland, full open-source). - **You need top-tier speeds.** Use NordVPN, Mullvad, or ExpressVPN. - **Your household has more than 4 people.** Use Surfshark (unlimited) or NordVPN (10 devices). - **You are on Linux or want router-level protection.** TunnelBear cannot help you. - **You want a guaranteed refund window.** Almost every competitor offers 30 days. ## Frequently Asked Questions **Is TunnelBear a trustworthy VPN?** TunnelBear is trustworthy within the limits of its corporate footprint. It has completed nine consecutive annual independent security audits with Cure53 (2017–2025) — the longest unbroken audit cadence of any major VPN. Its apps are partly open-source. Its no-logs policy has been repeatedly verified. The real trust concern is ownership: TunnelBear is owned by McAfee (US-based) and operates from Canada (Five Eyes member). That creates a larger attack surface for legal compulsion than jurisdictions like Switzerland (ProtonVPN) or Sweden (Mullvad) — but audits repeatedly confirm the company is not retaining the data a legal order would demand. **Is TunnelBear owned by McAfee?** Yes. McAfee acquired TunnelBear in March 2018. TunnelBear continues to operate as a semi-independent team within McAfee from Toronto, Canada. The original development team largely remained after the acquisition. TunnelBear's own blog post about joining McAfee openly acknowledged that US ownership "comes with increased privacy pressure and expectations" — transparency that is rare in post-acquisition announcements. **Is TunnelBear's free plan worth using in 2026?** For occasional use, yes. 2GB of monthly data is enough for a few hours of secure browsing on public Wi-Fi, light email, or checking bank accounts from hotels. Every app feature works on the free plan with AES-256 encryption, the VigilantBear kill switch, and access to all 47 countries in auto-select mode. However, as of January 2026 free users lost custom server selection and split tunneling. If you want a free VPN for daily use, Windscribe (10GB/month with email) or ProtonVPN Free (unlimited data on 5 countries) are better picks. **Does TunnelBear work with Netflix in 2026?** Unreliably. Netflix US, UK, Canada, Disney+, BBC iPlayer, Amazon Prime Video, and HBO Max all failed in our April 2026 tests. TunnelBear's own help center openly states that "issues streaming are not uncommon." Some reviewers (PCMag) report reliable unblocking; most others (Security.org, Cybernews, Comparitech, CyberInsider, PixelPrivacy) do not. If Netflix is your primary reason for buying a VPN, NordVPN, ExpressVPN, and Surfshark are dramatically more reliable. **Does TunnelBear track you?** TunnelBear's published no-logs policy covers IP addresses, DNS queries, traffic content, destinations, connection timestamps, and session durations. It does retain minimal operational data: OS version, app version, total bandwidth used (for free-tier enforcement), and whether the account connected at all in the last month. Every one of these items has been verified across nine consecutive Cure53 audits. This is more data than Mullvad collects but standard for commercial VPNs. **Why do I need to pay to use TunnelBear now?** You do not — the free plan still exists with 2GB/month, encryption, and all 47 countries. What changed in January 2026 is that free users lost custom server selection (servers are now auto-assigned) and split tunneling (SplitBear). TunnelBear explained the change as a cost-reduction move. Paid features previously shared with free users are now "behind the honeycomb wall." If you need those specific advanced features without paying, Windscribe's free tier keeps them available. **How long can you use TunnelBear for free?** Forever. The free plan does not expire, has no credit card requirement, and resets 2GB of data every calendar month. TunnelBear has not published a plan to sunset the free tier. Historically users could earn a one-time +1GB bonus by tweeting at the company, though the availability of this promotion varies. **Is TunnelBear safe on public Wi-Fi?** Yes. For its main use case — encrypting traffic on untrusted networks like cafes, airports, hotels, and co-working spaces — TunnelBear performs well. AES-256 encryption, VigilantBear kill switch, and verified no DNS / WebRTC / IPv6 leaks provide solid protection against the threats public Wi-Fi actually poses (packet sniffing, rogue hotspots, ARP spoofing). **Does TunnelBear work in China?** Unreliably. GhostBear, TunnelBear's obfuscation feature, is designed to disguise VPN traffic — but it is not optimized for the Great Firewall of China. For corporate firewalls, university networks, and moderately restrictive countries it works well. For China specifically, Astrill's Chameleon-style obfuscation or specialist tools have a stronger published track record. If you are travelling to mainland China, test before you go and have a backup plan. **How many devices can I connect with TunnelBear?** Five simultaneous connections on paid plans. The free plan has no hard device cap but everything shares the 2GB monthly data pool. Surfshark (unlimited), NordVPN (10), and ExpressVPN (8) all offer more connections. For a single user or small household TunnelBear is adequate. For a family of 5+ people with multiple devices each it is too restrictive. **Which VPN is better than TunnelBear?** It depends on your priority. For streaming, NordVPN and Surfshark are dramatically better. For privacy absolutism, Mullvad (Sweden, cash payments) and ProtonVPN (Switzerland, full open-source). For a better free plan, Windscribe (10GB/month). For unlimited devices on a budget, Surfshark. TunnelBear is best only if your specific priorities are audit discipline and beginner-friendliness. **Is TunnelBear selling my data?** No, based on nine consecutive Cure53 audits. The privacy policy and audit reports explicitly confirm TunnelBear does not sell user data, does not log browsing activity, and does not monetise user behaviour. The business model is paid subscriptions plus the now-smaller free tier, which is subsidised by paid users. The ad-tracking revenue model of sketchy free VPNs (Onavo, Hola, many free apps on Play Store) is not how TunnelBear operates. ## How TunnelBear Compares See how TunnelBear measures up in our head-to-head comparisons: - [**TunnelBear vs NordVPN**](/compare/tunnelbear-vs-nordvpn) — the mainstream market leader - [**TunnelBear vs Surfshark**](/compare/tunnelbear-vs-surfshark) — unlimited devices, cheaper plans - [**TunnelBear vs Windscribe**](/compare/tunnelbear-vs-windscribe) — fellow Canadian VPN with a more generous free tier ## Sources & References This review is based on our own hands-on testing of TunnelBear's Windows, macOS, Android, and iOS clients over April 2026, plus primary-source verification against the references below. Screenshots of the homepage, pricing page, and features page were captured on tunnelbear.com in April 2026. 1. [TunnelBear Official Website — tunnelbear.com](https://www.tunnelbear.com) 2.
